name: Polypropylene Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Polypropylene. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power range for Polypropylene surface cleaning without thermal degradation

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Polypropylene absorption characteristics and minimal thermal damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution on Polypropylene surfaces

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning while maintaining thermal control

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal while preserving Polypropylene substrate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants from Polypropylene surface

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage without excessive thermal buildup

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal from Polypropylene

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap percentage for uniform cleaning coverage

dwellTime.description: Optimal dwell time per spot for effective cleaning without thermal damage

power.description: Typical range for ns pulsed lasers at 1064 nm on polymers like PP to remove contaminants without melting; starting at 100W for light contamination. Max safe below 500W to avoid thermal damage (melting point ~165°C). Source: Adapted from IPG Photonics specs for YLR series and paper by Lorenz et al. (2016) on polymer laser cleaning.

scan_speed.description: Effective cleaning speeds for 1064 nm on PP; slower for heavy contamination (200-1000 mm/s) to ensure removal, faster for light (1000-3000 mm/s). Recommended 1000 mm/s balances efficiency and heat control given PP's low thermal conductivity (~0.1-0.2 W/mK). Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals and study by Anisimov et al. (2018) on plastic surface preparation.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for PP at 1064 nm to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) better for ultra-precise but costlier. 20 ns typical for ablation without excessive melting. Source: Laser physics research by Baudin et al. (2014) in Optics Express on polymer ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for cleaning applications; 50 kHz recommended for PP to achieve fast processing without heat accumulation (high freq increases overlap heat). Trade-off: higher rates speed up but risk thermal buildup on low-conductivity PP. Source: Academic literature from Kunze et al. (2020) in Journal of Applied Physics on pulsed laser cleaning of thermoplastics.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal on PP; damage threshold ~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to partial absorption (reflectivity ~0.4-0.6). Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe cleaning below threshold. Source: Ablation threshold studies by Bityurin et al. (2007) in Applied Surface Science on polypropylene laser interaction.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for cleaning; 100-300 μm for fine detail on PP, 300-1000 μm for large areas. 300 μm recommended for uniform coverage without pinpoint damage. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems for industrial cleaning.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy; 2 passes recommended for complete PP surface prep to ensure removal without overexposure. Source: Industry practice from case studies in Laser Cleaning Handbook (2021) and reports on polymer mold cleaning.

overlap_ratio.description: Typical 30-80% for uniform coverage; 60% recommended for PP to avoid gaps or excessive heat in adjacent lines, optimizing for even cleaning. Source: Process optimization studies by Strobl et al. (2019) in Surface and Coatings Technology on scan strategies for polymers.
